findings.title: POST /auth/logout emits no audit row — session termination is untracked
findings.description: The logout handler revokes the refresh token and blacklists the JTI, but calls neither `auditService.log()` nor `auditPersistence.log()`. No `LOGOUT` action exists in the `AuditAction` enum. An auditor cannot reconstruct which sessions were voluntarily terminated versus which tokens simply expired, and cannot correlate "user A logged in at T1 and logged out at T2" from the audit trail alone.
findings.root_cause: No audit emission was ever added to the logout controller path, and no LOGOUT enum member was defined in AuditAction.
findings.impact: Session lifecycle is not fully observable. Forensic analysis of a compromised account cannot determine if the legitimate user was still logged in at a given point, or if an ongoing session was terminated.
findings.suggested_fix: 1. Add `LOGOUT = 'LOGOUT'` to the `AuditAction` enum in `audit-action.enum.ts`. 2. Add it to the `AUDIT_CATEGORY_MAP` in `audit.service.ts`. 3. In `AuthController.logout()`, call `this.auditService.log({ action: AuditAction.LOGOUT, userId: user.id, ip: clientIp(req), ua: req.headers?.['user-agent'], details: ..., success: true })` after the blacklist step.
acceptance_criteria: A row with action='LOGOUT' appears in audit_logs (or the console SecurityAudit sink) after POST /auth/logout
The row captures the actor userId and the originating IP/UA
findings.notes: The AuditAction enum has no LOGOUT member; the gap is structural, not just a missing call.

findings.title: POST /users (admin-side user creation) emits no audit row
findings.description: After the `prisma.user.create()` call in `UsersService.create()`, no `auditPersistence.log()` or `auditService.log()` is invoked. Self-registration via `/auth/register` does emit a `REGISTER` audit, but the admin-side POST /users creation (which creates an immediately-active account) generates no audit entry. This means account provisioning by administrators is invisible in the audit trail.
findings.root_cause: The `create()` method never received an audit emit, while the focus was on update (role change, deactivation, service membership) and delete paths.
findings.impact: An admin who provisions a user account leaves no audit trail. A compromised admin account could create privileged users without any forensic evidence in `audit_logs`. RGPD accountability for account creation is absent for the admin path.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `USER_CREATED = 'USER_CREATED'` to AuditAction. After the `prisma.user.create()` and the optional `userService.createMany()`, emit: `await this.auditPersistence.log({ action: AuditAction.USER_CREATED, entityType: 'User', entityId: user.id, actorId: caller?.id ?? null, payload: { after: { roleId: user.roleId, departmentId: user.departmentId, isActive: user.isActive } } })`. The caller id should be threaded from the controller.
acceptance_criteria: A durable audit_logs row with action='USER_CREATED' appears after POST /users
The actorId field is the creating admin's id
findings.notes: The update path has thorough auditing (ROLE_CHANGE, USER_DEACTIVATED, SERVICE_MEMBERSHIP_CHANGED, DEPARTMENT_CHANGED); the create path was never similarly instrumented.

findings.title: POST /users/import bulk-creates users with no audit row per created user
findings.code_evidence:
const user = await this.prisma.user.create({
          data: {
            email: userData.email,
            login: userData.login,
            passwordHash,
            firstName: userData.firstName,
            lastName: userData.lastName,
            roleId,
            departmentId,
            isActive: true,
          },
findings.description: In `importUsers()`, each successfully created user triggers a `prisma.user.create()` call inside a per-row try/catch loop. No `auditPersistence.log()` or `auditService.log()` is called for any created user. A bulk import of N users leaves N user provisioning events entirely untracked in the audit trail.
findings.root_cause: The import path was written independently of the single-user create path and inherited the same missing audit instrumentation.
findings.impact: Bulk user provisioning (e.g. at system roll-out or when onboarding a new department) is unaudited. Combined with a compromised admin, this creates a silent attack vector for mass account creation.
findings.suggested_fix: After each successful `prisma.user.create()` in the import loop, emit `auditPersistence.log({ action: AuditAction.USER_CREATED, entityType: 'User', entityId: user.id, actorId: callerRoleCode !== undefined ? ..., payload: { importedRow: i+1, roleId: user.roleId } })`. A batch-level summary audit row (action: SYSTEM_BACKFILL or a dedicated USERS_IMPORTED) would also be acceptable and less verbose.
acceptance_criteria: After POST /users/import, at least one audit_logs row exists per created user referencing the action and the new user id

findings.title: Leave create path emits no durable audit row — only transient console log for self-approve
findings.description: The `create()` method has three outcome paths: (1) PENDING leave submission, (2) declaredByManager (immediately APPROVED), (3) canSelfApprove (immediately APPROVED). None of the three calls `auditPersistence.log()`. Path (3) calls `auditService.log()` (console/SecurityAudit only — not persisted to `audit_logs`), and even that is wrong because it emits LEAVE_APPROVED rather than LEAVE_CREATED. Paths (1) and (2) emit nothing at all. The full leave lifecycle (update, delete, approve, reject, cancel) is audited, but the creation event is entirely absent from the durable audit trail.
findings.root_cause: The create() path was instrumented for the status-transition events (approve, reject) but a LEAVE_CREATED emission was never added.
findings.impact: An auditor cannot establish when a leave request was submitted, who submitted it, or whether a manager-declared leave on behalf of another user was authorised at that moment. This is a compliance gap for RGPD (right to justification) and HR policy (override visibility).
findings.suggested_fix: Add `LEAVE_CREATED = 'LEAVE_CREATED'` to AuditAction. After the transaction that creates the leave row, emit `auditPersistence.log({ action: AuditAction.LEAVE_CREATED, entityType: 'Leave', entityId: leave.id, actorId: requestingUserId, payload: { userId, declaredByManager, initialStatus, leaveTypeId, startDate: leave.startDate, endDate: leave.endDate, days: leave.days } })`. This should replace the current `auditService.log()` call and cover all three paths.
acceptance_criteria: After POST /leaves, a durable audit_logs row with action='LEAVE_CREATED' exists
The payload captures the actor, the target user, and the initial status
findings.notes: The declaredByManager=true path creates an immediately APPROVED leave with no audit of either the creation or the approval decision. This is the most serious gap of the three create paths.

findings.title: rejectCancellation() (CANCELLATION_REQUESTED → APPROVED) emits no audit row
findings.description: The `rejectCancellation()` method transitions a leave from CANCELLATION_REQUESTED back to APPROVED. All other leave status transitions (approve, reject, cancel, requestCancel, update) emit a durable `auditPersistence.log()` call. This method is the sole exception: the manager decision to deny a cancellation request and restore the leave to APPROVED is silently not recorded in `audit_logs`. Notably the similar `cancel()` method (lines 2061–2138) emits LEAVE_CANCELLED with before/after state.
findings.root_cause: The `rejectCancellation()` method was added (COR-009) to complete the cancellation state machine but its audit emit was not included, unlike the symmetric `cancel()` path.
findings.impact: A manager who denies a cancellation request and thereby forces an employee to take a leave they wanted to cancel leaves no audit trace. In case of a dispute, there is no record of this decision.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `LEAVE_CANCELLATION_REJECTED = 'LEAVE_CANCELLATION_REJECTED'` to AuditAction (or reuse LEAVE_REJECTED with a before.status=CANCELLATION_REQUESTED marker). Inside the `$transaction`, after `tx.leave.update()`, emit `await this.auditPersistence.log({ action: AuditAction.LEAVE_CANCELLATION_REJECTED, entityType: 'Leave', entityId: id, actorId: currentUserId ?? null, payload: { before: { status: current.status }, after: { status: updated.status }, targetUserId: current.userId } })`.
acceptance_criteria: After PATCH /leaves/:id/reject-cancellation, a durable audit_logs row records the before (CANCELLATION_REQUESTED) and after (APPROVED) states
The actorId field is the manager's user id

findings.title: createDelegation() and deactivateDelegation() emit no audit row
findings.description: Delegations are RBAC-adjacent sensitive actions: they transfer leave approval authority from one user (delegator) to another (delegate) for a time window. Both `createDelegation()` (lines 2329–2407) and `deactivateDelegation()` (lines 2454–2485) perform state-changing DB writes with no `auditPersistence.log()` or `auditService.log()` call. A delegator could silently transfer their approval authority to any user in the system without any trace.
findings.root_cause: Delegation management was implemented without audit instrumentation.
findings.impact: Audit trail for leave approval authority delegation is completely absent. This is a compliance and forensic gap: if a delegation is abused to approve fraudulent leaves, the delegation lifecycle is invisible.
findings.suggested_fix: Emit `auditPersistence.log({ action: AuditAction.LEAVE_BALANCE_ADJUSTED, ... })` — or better, add `DELEGATION_CREATED` and `DELEGATION_DEACTIVATED` to AuditAction — in both `createDelegation()` and `deactivateDelegation()`. The payload should include delegatorId, delegateId, startDate, endDate, and actorId (the requesting user).
acceptance_criteria: A durable audit_logs row exists after creating a delegation
A durable audit_logs row exists after deactivating a delegation
Both rows capture the delegator, delegate, and the actor performing the operation

findings.title: Project create, update, and soft-delete (status→CANCELLED) emit no audit rows
findings.description: Only the `archive()`, `unarchive()`, and `hardDelete()` methods emit audit rows. The three core lifecycle methods are unaudited: `create()` (lines 89–221) creates a project and silently adds the creator as a member with no audit; `update()` (lines 548–662) modifies arbitrary project fields including status, manager, and sponsor with no before/after audit; `remove()` (lines 667–687) transitions a project to CANCELLED status with no audit. This means the vast majority of project lifecycle events are invisible in the audit trail.
findings.root_cause: Audit was retroactively added only to the archive/unarchive/hardDelete paths; the foundational CRUD operations were never instrumented.
findings.impact: A project manager can create, rename, change the managing team, and cancel a project with no forensic evidence in audit_logs. Status changes (DRAFT→IN_PROGRESS→COMPLETED→CANCELLED) are untracked. For a government HR/project management system this is a significant accountability gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Add PROJECT_CREATED, PROJECT_UPDATED, PROJECT_CANCELLED to AuditAction. In create(): emit PROJECT_CREATED with the project id and creatorId. In update(): capture the before-state for key fields (name, status, managerId, sponsorId) and emit PROJECT_UPDATED with before/after diff. In remove(): emit PROJECT_CANCELLED.
acceptance_criteria: After POST /projects, an audit_logs row with action='PROJECT_CREATED' exists
After PATCH /projects/:id, an audit_logs row with action='PROJECT_UPDATED' and before/after state exists
After DELETE /projects/:id, an audit_logs row with action='PROJECT_CANCELLED' exists

findings.title: Project member add/update/remove emit no audit rows
findings.description: The three project membership methods — `addMember()` (lines 891–951), `updateMember()` (lines 957–1003), and `removeMember()` (lines 1008–1041) — perform DB writes with no audit emission. Project membership defines who has access to a project and in what role (allocation, project role). Adding/removing members changes the project's permission perimeter but is untracked.
findings.root_cause: Project membership management never received audit instrumentation.
findings.impact: A project owner can add or remove team members without any audit record. For accountability over who had project access at a given time this is a gap, though less critical than the lifecycle events in O2-7.
findings.suggested_fix: Add PROJECT_MEMBER_ADDED / PROJECT_MEMBER_REMOVED / PROJECT_MEMBER_UPDATED to AuditAction (or reuse SERVICE_MEMBERSHIP_CHANGED pattern). Emit in each method after the DB write.
acceptance_criteria: After POST /projects/:id/members, an audit_logs row with action='PROJECT_MEMBER_ADDED' exists

findings.title: Task create, update, and delete emit no audit rows — only CSV export is audited
findings.description: In `TasksService`, only the `exportProjectTasksCsv()` method emits an audit row (`DATA_EXPORTED`). The `create()` method (task creation), `update()` (status changes, assignee changes, priority changes, all sensitive in project governance), and `remove()` (task deletion) all perform DB writes without any audit emission. Task status transitions (TODO→IN_PROGRESS→DONE) represent project execution state changes that are material for project governance and compliance.
findings.root_cause: The audit instrumentation strategy focused on higher-sensitivity domains (leaves, RBAC, users) and task operations were not included.
findings.impact: Project task lifecycle is entirely absent from the audit trail. A contributor can change task status, reassign tasks, or delete tasks without any record. For a government project management system tracking deliverables, this is an accountability gap.
findings.suggested_fix: At minimum, add audit rows for status changes in `update()` (TASK_STATUS_CHANGED: before.status, after.status, taskId, projectId) and for `remove()` (TASK_DELETED: task snapshot). Add corresponding AuditAction members.
acceptance_criteria: After PATCH /tasks/:id with a status change, an audit_logs row records the status transition
After DELETE /tasks/:id, an audit_logs row records the deletion
findings.notes: The AuditPersistenceService is injected in TasksService but only used for the export path, confirming the gap is intentional omission rather than a missing injection.

findings.title: generateResetToken() logs PASSWORD_CHANGED (wrong action) via console-only sink
findings.description: The `generateResetToken()` path calls `auditService.log()` (which goes to the console SecurityAudit sink and is NOT persisted to `audit_logs`) and misclassifies the event as `PASSWORD_CHANGED`. No password was changed at this point — only a reset token was issued. The correct action would be something like `PASSWORD_RESET_TOKEN_ISSUED`. Additionally, `auditService.log()` does not write to `audit_logs`; an admin issuing a reset token leaves no durable audit trail.
findings.root_cause: The action code was copied from the `resetPassword()` path without adapting it to the token-issuance event, and the console-only sink was used instead of `auditPersistence.log()`.
findings.impact: An admin who issues a reset token for a privileged user account leaves no durable audit trail. Combined with the fact that the token gives full account access, this is a material accountability gap. The mislabeled action also pollutes analytics querying for `PASSWORD_CHANGED` events.
findings.suggested_fix: 1. Add `PASSWORD_RESET_TOKEN_ISSUED = 'PASSWORD_RESET_TOKEN_ISSUED'` to AuditAction. 2. Replace `auditService.log()` with `auditPersistence.log()` (durable). 3. Use the new action. The payload should record `actorId: createdById`, `entityId: targetUser.id`, `entityType: 'User'`.
acceptance_criteria: After POST /auth/reset-password-token, a durable audit_logs row with action='PASSWORD_RESET_TOKEN_ISSUED' exists
No action='PASSWORD_CHANGED' row is emitted by this path

auth/auth.service.ts: login failure → durable AuditAction.LOGIN_FAILURE (auditService.log)
auth/auth.service.ts: account locked → durable AuditAction.ACCOUNT_LOCKED with before/after (auditService.log)
auth/auth.service.ts: register → AuditAction.REGISTER (auditService.log)
auth/auth.service.ts: resetPassword (token consumption) → AuditAction.PASSWORD_CHANGED (auditService.log)
users/users.service.ts: update with role change → AuditAction.ROLE_CHANGE durable with before/after roleCode (auditPersistence.log)
users/users.service.ts: update with isActive false → AuditAction.USER_DEACTIVATED durable with before/after (auditPersistence.log)
users/users.service.ts: update with isActive true → AuditAction.USER_REACTIVATED durable with before/after (auditPersistence.log)
users/users.service.ts: update with departmentId change → AuditAction.DEPARTMENT_CHANGED durable (auditPersistence.log)
users/users.service.ts: update with serviceIds change → AuditAction.SERVICE_MEMBERSHIP_CHANGED durable with added/removed diff (auditPersistence.log)
users/users.service.ts: remove (soft-delete) → AuditAction.USER_DEACTIVATED durable (auditPersistence.log)
users/users.service.ts: changePassword → AuditAction.PASSWORD_CHANGED durable with before/after forcePasswordChange (auditPersistence.log)
users/users.service.ts: resetPassword (admin) → AuditAction.PASSWORD_RESET_BY_ADMIN durable with before/after updatedAt (auditPersistence.log)
users/users.service.ts: hardDelete → AuditAction.USER_DELETED durable with column snapshot before erasure (auditPersistence.log)
rbac/roles.service.ts: createRole → AuditAction.ROLE_CREATED durable with after-state (auditPersistence.log)
rbac/roles.service.ts: updateRole → AuditAction.ROLE_UPDATED durable with before/after (auditPersistence.log) — no-op DTOs emit nothing
rbac/roles.service.ts: deleteRole → AuditAction.ROLE_DELETED durable with snapshot before delete (auditPersistence.log)
rbac/roles.service.ts: default role change → AuditAction.ROLE_DEFAULT_CHANGED durable with before/after roleId (auditPersistence.log)
leaves/leaves.service.ts: approve → AuditAction.LEAVE_APPROVED durable with actor/subject snapshots inside tx (auditPersistence.log)
leaves/leaves.service.ts: reject → AuditAction.LEAVE_REJECTED durable with actor/subject snapshots inside tx (auditPersistence.log)
leaves/leaves.service.ts: cancel → AuditAction.LEAVE_CANCELLED durable with before/after status inside tx (auditPersistence.log)
leaves/leaves.service.ts: requestCancel → AuditAction.LEAVE_CANCELLATION_REQUESTED durable with actor snapshot inside tx (auditPersistence.log)
leaves/leaves.service.ts: update → AuditAction.LEAVE_UPDATED durable with before/after dates/days inside tx (auditPersistence.log)
leaves/leaves.service.ts: remove (hard delete) → AuditAction.LEAVE_DELETED durable with full snapshot (auditPersistence.log)
leaves/leaves.service.ts: upsertBalance → AuditAction.LEAVE_BALANCE_ADJUSTED durable with before/after totalDays and operation (auditPersistence.log)
leaves/leaves.service.ts: deleteBalance → AuditAction.LEAVE_BALANCE_ADJUSTED durable with operation='DELETE' (auditPersistence.log)

projects/projects.service.ts: unarchive → AuditAction.PROJECT_UNARCHIVED durable inside tx (auditPersistence.log)
projects/projects.service.ts: hardDelete → AuditAction.PROJECT_DELETED durable with column snapshot before delete (auditPersistence.log)
